Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a proper study routine. I'd just kind of drift through the week and study whenever I remembered or felt like it, which wasn't often. At 27, I've got other things pulling at my attention, and motivation has been a real struggle. I'd usually end up cramming things in when I realized deadlines were approaching, listening to lectures while doing other stuff around the house.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. I'd go days without touching course material, then suddenly spend hours catching up when something was due. The only consistent thing was inconsistency. I relied heavily on deadlines to push me into action because otherwise I just couldn't find the drive to start.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or getting a notification that something was due soon. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, scrolling through without really engaging. The anxiety of potentially failing something would push me to at least show up, even when I didn't feel like actually learning.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became a natural routine for me. There were brief periods where I'd log in more regularly, usually when assignments were clustered together. But as soon as the pressure lifted, I'd drift away again. It felt more like a chore I had to check off than something I wanted to do.
Q5: The lecture recordings were the most useful for me since I learn better by listening. I could play them while doing other things and absorb information that way. The audio format worked well with my learning style, and I could replay confusing parts without feeling embarrassed like I would in a live class.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums entirely. I never posted anything because the thought of putting my thoughts out there for others to judge made my anxiety spike. Reading materials that were just walls of text got skipped a lot too—I'd open them, skim the first paragraph, and close them, telling myself I'd come back later.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or exams. If I couldn't see an immediate payoff, my brain just wouldn't engage with it. With my motivation being so low, I needed that clear link to something concrete, otherwise it felt like I was just going through motions for no reason.
Q8: Optional activities might as well have been invisible to me. If something wasn't required, I simply didn't do it. Required activities at least had consequences attached, which gave my anxious brain enough pressure to actually complete them. Without that external push, I just couldn't generate the internal motivation.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of lecture audio files and listened to them on my phone away from the computer. I'd also take notes by hand while listening, which helped things stick better. Sometimes I'd discuss concepts with a friend who was taking similar courses, and none of that showed up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or replaying downloaded lectures during commutes or while cooking. I'd work through problems on paper, talk through concepts out loud to myself. The LMS would show nothing during those periods, but I was actually processing and learning the material in my own way.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download lecture recordings once and then listen to them multiple times offline over the following days. Same with any PDF materials—I'd save them and read them on my tablet later. The LMS only saw that initial download, not the hours I spent with those materials afterward.
Q12: There were stretches where everything felt pointless and I just couldn't make myself care about the course. The sadness would settle in and even simple tasks felt overwhelming. My anxiety would be screaming at me about falling behind while my motivation was completely flatlined. Those were dark periods where I'd barely touch anything.
Q13: The workload from all six online courses sometimes piled up in ways that made me shut down entirely. When I got confused about something, instead of seeking help, I'd just avoid it because reaching out felt too anxiety-inducing. Fatigue was constant—I'd stare at the screen and nothing would register, so I'd just click through things mindlessly.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely, lost in anxious thoughts or just feeling empty. I'd scroll through materials without actually reading them, just going through the motions. My body was present but my brain had checked out completely.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts indefinitely because the social anxiety around them was too much. I'd also avoid starting assignments until the last possible moment because beginning something felt harder than finishing it. I knew these things mattered, but the anxiety and low motivation created this paralysis I couldn't break through easily.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me engage with the LMS consistently. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the ability to focus that had been missing all week. My activity would spike dramatically in the 24-48 hours before something was due, then drop off completely afterward.
Q17: When I got feedback, it would affect me for a day or two. Positive feedback gave me a brief mood boost, but it never translated into sustained motivation. Critical feedback would send my anxiety through the roof, and I'd avoid looking at that assignment again. The CBT sessions have been helping me process feedback more constructively.
Q18: The structure with clear deadlines helped me because I needed those external markers to function. But the expectation of discussion participation was rough for someone with my anxiety levels. Having everything available online was convenient, but it also made it too easy to keep putting things off since nothing forced me to show up at a specific time.
Q19: The audio and video content made engagement easier given my learning style. What made it harder was anything requiring me to interact with other students—that triggered my anxiety badly. The flexibility was both a blessing and curse; I appreciated being able to work at my own pace, but I also needed more structure than I gave myself.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—clicking around, opening things, technically 'active' but not actually absorbing anything. They might be anxious about missing something so they check constantly, but when it comes to producing work, they freeze up. High activity doesn't mean high engagement; sometimes it's just anxious monitoring.
Q21: That actually describes me somewhat. I did okay on exams and assignments despite not being super active on the LMS because I did a lot of learning offline. Listening to downloaded lectures, taking handwritten notes, processing things in my own time. The LMS couldn't see any of that, but it was real studying.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, whether you meet deadlines. But they can't show if you're actually learning or just going through motions. They miss all the offline work, the emotional state behind the clicks, whether someone is engaged or just anxious. They capture behavior, not understanding or effort.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just get through it. It's when your mind is present and you're making connections, asking questions internally, feeling curious. For me, true engagement has been rare this course—mostly I've been surviving rather than thriving, just trying to pass.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I struggled emotionally just to complete basic tasks. It can't capture the anxiety attacks before posting something or the sadness that made getting out of bed hard. It doesn't show the resilience it took to keep going despite feeling disconnected from everything. My numbers look decent, but they don't reflect the internal battle behind them.
